title: Course Curriculum & Syllabus
text:
2. Course Rationale & Objectives
This intensive 20-hour foundation course is designed to transition engineering students from foundational mathematics to deploying modern Generative AI applications. Structured strictly within the parameters of Outcome-Based Education (OBE), the curriculum emphasizes a rigorous mathematical foundation followed by practical implementation. The objective is to cultivate analytical reasoning and engineering problem-solving capabilities required for contemporary AI engineering roles.

title: Course Curriculum & Syllabus
text:
3. Course Outcomes (COs)
Upon successful completion of the course, students will be able to:

CO1: Apply foundational principles of linear algebra, calculus, and probability to formulate machine learning models and optimization strategies. (Cognitive Level: Apply)
CO2: Implement and evaluate supervised and unsupervised classical machine learning algorithms using standard Python frameworks and structured datasets. (Cognitive Level: Apply/Analyze)
CO3: Develop deep learning architectures, analyzing the performance impact of activation functions (such as ELU, ReLU, and SiLU) and loss configurations. (Cognitive Level: Create/Evaluate)
CO4: Design and deploy end-to-end generative AI pipelines, including Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, utilizing modern deployment frameworks. (Cognitive Level: Create)

title: Course Curriculum & Syllabus
text:
5. Detailed Syllabus Structure

Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI (4 Hours)

Linear Algebra: Vector spaces, matrix operations, Orthogonality, Eigenvalues/Eigenvectors, Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
Calculus: Partial derivatives, the Chain rule, gradient intuition for backpropagation.
Optimization: Gradient Descent mechanics, Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), Adam Optimizer introduction.
Probability: Bayesian vs. Frequentist probability, Gaussian distributions, basics of hypothesis testing.
Hands-on Component: Python implementation of a Gradient Descent algorithm from scratch to visualize optimization paths.



Module 2: Classical Machine Learning & Data Workflows (6 Hours)

Data Lifecycle: GitHub fundamentals for collaboration; Kaggle API for data sourcing; Data cleaning and Feature Engineering.
Supervised Learning: Regression (Linear, Polynomial) and Classification (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost).
Unsupervised Learning: K-Means and Hierarchical clustering methodologies.
Dimensionality Reduction: Principles of PCA vs. t-SNE for high-dimensional data visualization.
Hands-on Component: End-to-end exploratory data analysis and ML pipeline construction using Scikit-Learn on benchmarking datasets.



Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures (4 Hours)

Neural Network Architecture: Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLP) topology and forward propagation.
Activation & Loss: Comparative analysis of activation functions (ReLU, ELU, SiLU) and their impact on performance metrics like FID scores. Loss functions (MSE, Cross-entropy).
Training Dynamics: Overfitting vs. Underfitting mitigation strategies including Dropout and Early Stopping.
Frameworks: Building, compiling, and evaluating models utilizing Keras and TensorFlow backends.
Hands-on Component: Designing and training a deep neural network for a classification task.



Module 4: Natural Language Processing & Generative AI (4 Hours)

NLP Foundations: Evolution from sequential models to Transformers; understanding the Self-Attention mechanism.
Large Language Models (LLMs): GPT architecture fundamentals and Prompt Engineering methodologies (Few-shot, Chain-of-Thought).
Modern AI Stack: Interfacing with the Hugging Face ecosystem (pre-trained models and pipelines).
RAG Systems: Principles of Retrieval-Augmented Generation utilizing NotebookLM methodologies and vector stores.
Hands-on Component: Developing a local RAG-based chatbot for technical documentation querying.



Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment (2 Hours)

Model Deployment: Principles of ModelOps and lightweight web application frameworks for data science.
Streamlit Integration: Transforming trained models (ML or GenAI) into interactive web applications.
Deliverable Configuration: Structuring and finalizing GitHub repositories with comprehensive README documentation.
Hands-on Component: Guided deployment of the final Capstone project for portfolio presentation.

title: Course Curriculum & Syllabus
text:
6. Assessment & Evaluation Strategy (OBE Compliant)






Quality Assurance & Alignment



To maintain rigorous quality assurance and alignment with accreditation standards, the evaluation scheme utilizes both formative and summative assessments mapped directly to the Course Outcomes.



Continuous Assessment (30% - Mapped to CO1, CO2): Automated quizzes and conceptual checks focusing on the mathematical frameworks and classical algorithm mechanics.
Practical Execution (30% - Mapped to CO2, CO3): Evaluation of “Skeleton Notebooks” where foundational logic and framework implementations (e.g., proper application of activation functions and optimizers) are assessed.
Capstone Project (40% - Mapped to CO4, PO3, PO5): Summative evaluation of a deployed, interactive Streamlit application. Rubrics will evaluate code modularity, Git commit history, and model accuracy/utility.

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
The Jupyter Notebook Trap
Training a model with 99% accuracy is only 50% of the job. A machine learning model trapped inside a Jupyter Notebook is useless to the end-user.
ModelOps (Model Operations): The lifecycle of taking a trained model, wrapping it in an accessible interface (API or Web UI), and deploying it to the internet.

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
The Deployment Pipeline

Serialize (Save): Export your trained model from memory to a file (e.g., using joblib for Scikit-Learn or .h5 for Keras).
Interface: Build a web application that takes user input and feeds it to the loaded model.
Host: Push the code to a cloud server (GitHub Pages, Streamlit Cloud, AWS).

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
What is Streamlit?
Streamlit is an open-source Python framework designed specifically for Machine Learning engineers. It allows you to build highly interactive web applications in pure Python, without needing to know any HTML, CSS, or JavaScript.
How it works: Every time a user interacts with a widget (like clicking a button or moving a slider), Streamlit entirely reruns your Python script from top to bottom.

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
Basic Streamlit Syntax
Streamlit commands are incredibly intuitive.
import streamlit as st

# Text Elements
st.title("My First AI App")
st.write("Welcome to my Capstone Project!")

# Input Widgets
age = st.slider("Select your age:", 18, 100)
name = st.text_input("Enter your name:")

# Actions
if st.button("Submit"):
    st.success(f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old.")

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
The app.py Architecture
To deploy our model, we create a single app.py file.
import streamlit as st
import joblib
import numpy as np

# 1. Load the pre-trained model
model = joblib.load('random_forest_model.pkl')

st.title("House Price Predictor 🏠")

# 2. Get user input
sqft = st.number_input("Square Footage", min_value=500, max_value=10000)
bedrooms = st.selectbox("Bedrooms", [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])

# 3. Make Prediction
if st.button("Predict Price"):
    features = np.array([[sqft, bedrooms]])
    prediction = model.predict(features)[0]
    
    st.balloons()
    st.success(f"Estimated Price: ${prediction:,.2f}")

title: Module 5: Capstone Integration & Deployment
text:
Running the App
Unlike Jupyter Notebooks, you run Streamlit from the command line (or Colab terminal).
# Install the library
pip install streamlit

# Run the server
streamlit run app.py
This will launch a local web server and open the app in your browser!

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
What is Natural Language Processing?
Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the field of AI that enables computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language.
Everyday Examples You Already Use: * Google Translate - Converting text between languages * Siri/Alexa - Understanding voice commands * Spam Filters - Detecting unwanted emails * Autocorrect - Predicting your next word * ChatGPT - Generating human-like responses

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Why is NLP Challenging?
Human language is messy and complex:

Ambiguity: “I saw a man on a hill with a telescope.” (Who has the telescope?)
Context: “It’s cold in here.” (Could be a request to close the window)
Sarcasm: “Great, another meeting!” (Means the opposite)
Idioms: “Break a leg!” (Not literal!)

The Core Challenge: How do we convert unstructured text (words, sentences) into something a computer can process mathematically?

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
The Problem with Early Approaches
Before 2017, NLP relied on Recurrence Neural Networks (RNNs) and LSTMs.
How they worked: They processed text one word at a time, left to right, like reading a book.
The Flaws: 1. The Forgetting Problem: By the end of a long sentence, they “forgot” the beginning. Like trying to remember the first word of a paragraph after reading the whole page. 2. Slow Training: Because each word depends on the previous one, you can’t speed up the process. Training took weeks or months even on powerful computers.

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
The Transformer Revolution (2017)
Google researchers published a groundbreaking paper: “Attention Is All You Need”
The Key Insight: Instead of reading word-by-word, what if we could look at the entire sentence at once and figure out which words are most related to each other?

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Understanding Self-Attention (The Core Idea)
Analogy: Imagine you’re reading this sentence:

“The animal didn’t cross the street because it was too wide.”

When you see the word “it”, your brain automatically asks: “What does ‘it’ refer to?” You scan the sentence and realize “it” = “the street.”
Self-Attention does the same thing mathematically: * It looks at every word in the sentence simultaneously * It calculates how strongly each word relates to every other word * It creates a “attention map” showing these relationships
Why this matters: The model can now understand context and relationships regardless of word distance!

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
section: What is a Large Language Model?
text:
What is a Large Language Model?
An LLM is a massive AI model trained on huge amounts of text data (books, websites, articles, etc.).
Examples: ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Claude, Meta Llama
How they work (simplified): 1. Training: The model reads billions of sentences and learns patterns 2. Learning Objective: Predict the next word in a sequence * Example: “The cat sat on the ___” → Model learns to predict “mat” or “couch” 3. Emergent Abilities: By learning to predict words, the model inadvertently learns: * Grammar and syntax * Facts about the world * Basic reasoning * Even some coding ability!

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Prompt Engineering: Getting Better Results
Since LLMs are essentially “text completion engines,” how you ask matters!
Technique 1: Zero-Shot Prompting
Just ask your question directly:
What is the capital of France?
Technique 2: Few-Shot Prompting
Give examples of what you want:
Convert these to formal language:
Informal: "Hey, what's up?"
Formal: "Hello, how are you?"

Informal: "Gotta go, bye!"
Formal: "I must leave now, goodbye."

Informal: "Can you help me?"
Formal: 
Technique 3: Chain-of-Thought
Ask the model to think step by step:
Question: If I have 3 apples and buy 5 more, then give away 2, how many do I have?

Let's think step by step:
1. Start with 3 apples
2. Buy 5 more: 3 + 5 = 8 apples
3. Give away 2: 8 - 2 = 6 apples
Answer: 6 apples
Why this works: It forces the model to show its reasoning, reducing errors in math and logic problems.

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
The Hugging Face Ecosystem
Hugging Face is like “GitHub for AI Models” - a platform where researchers and companies share pre-trained models.
Why this matters for you: * You don’t need to train models from scratch (which costs millions) * You can download and use state-of-the-art models in a few lines of code * Huge community support and documentation

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Simple Example: Sentiment Analysis
Let’s see how easy it is to use a pre-trained model:
#| eval: false
from transformers import pipeline

# Load a pre-trained sentiment analysis model in ONE line
classifier = pipeline("sentiment-analysis")

# Test it
result = classifier("I love building AI models!")
print(result)
# Output: [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.9998}]

result = classifier("This is terrible and I hate it.")
print(result)
# Output: [{'label': 'NEGATIVE', 'score': 0.9997}]
What just happened? 1. We downloaded a model that was already trained on millions of movie reviews 2. We used it to classify text as positive or negative 3. All in 3 lines of code!

Real-World Applications

Product Reviews: Analyze customer feedback at scale

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
The Hallucination Problem
LLMs have two major limitations:
1. Knowledge Cutoff: They only know what was in their training data. * GPT-4’s knowledge ends around April 2023 * It doesn’t know about recent events, new research, or your company’s latest products
2. No Access to Private Data: They can’t see your company documents, emails, or databases.
The Result: When you ask about something they don’t know, they often confidently make things up - this is called “hallucination.”

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
RAG: The Solution
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines LLMs with an external knowledge base.
How it works (3 steps):

Prepare Your Documents: Convert your documents (PDFs, websites, etc.) into a searchable format
Retrieve: When you ask a question, find the most relevant documents from your knowledge base
Generate: Feed those documents to the LLM as context, so it can give an accurate answer

Analogy: Think of RAG as giving the LLM an “open book exam” - it can look up information before answering.

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
RAG in Action
Use Case: A company chatbot that answers questions about internal policies.
#| eval: false
# Simplified RAG example
from transformers import pipeline
from pinecone import PineconeClient

# Initialize the LLM and vector database
generator = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt-3.5-turbo")
pinecone_client = PineconeClient(api_key="your-key")

def rag_query(query):
    # Step 1: Find relevant documents
    retrieved_docs = pinecone_client.query(query, top_k=3)
    context = " ".join([doc['text'] for doc in retrieved_docs])
    
    # Step 2: Generate answer using retrieved context
    response = generator(f"""
    Use the following context to answer the question.
    
    Context: {context}
    
    Question: {query}
    
    Answer:""")
    return response

# Example: Ask about company policy
print(rag_query("What is our vacation policy?"))
What’s happening: 1. The system searches your company handbook for “vacation policy” 2. It finds the relevant section 3. It gives that section to the LLM 4. The LLM generates an accurate answer based on your actual policy

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Building a Simple RAG Pipeline
Let’s build a basic RAG system that can answer questions about technical documentation.
Step 1: Prepare the Knowledge Base
#| eval: false
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# Our "documents" - in reality, these would be from your PDFs, websites, etc.
documents = [
    "The learning rate for the Adam optimizer is set to 0.001.",
    "Dropout is used at 20% to prevent overfitting.",
    "The model utilizes the SiLU activation function.",
    "Training was performed on NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 48 hours.",
    "The dataset contains 50,000 training samples and 10,000 test samples."
]
Step 2: Convert Text to Vectors (Embeddings)
#| eval: false
# Load a model that converts text to numbers (embeddings)
embedder = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

# Convert all documents to vectors
doc_vectors = embedder.encode(documents)

print(f"Document vector shape: {doc_vectors.shape}")
# Each document is now a list of 384 numbers!
What are embeddings? * They’re numerical representations of text * Similar texts have similar vectors * We can use math to find “similar” documents
Step 3: Find Relevant Documents for a Query
#| eval: false
# The user's question
query = "What activation function is used?"

# Convert query to vector
query_vector = embedder.encode([query])

# Find the most similar document (using cosine similarity)
similarities = np.dot(doc_vectors, query_vector.T).flatten()
best_match_idx = np.argmax(similarities)

print(f"Query: {query}")
print(f"Most relevant document: {documents[best_match_idx]}")
Step 4: Generate the Final Answer
#| eval: false
# In a real system, we would now pass the retrieved document to an LLM
# to generate a natural language answer

retrieved_context = documents[best_match_idx]

# Simple template-based answer (in practice, use an LLM)
answer = f"Based on the documentation: {retrieved_context}"
print(f"Answer: {answer}")

title: Module 4: NLP & Generative AI
text:
Key Takeaways from This Demo

Text → Numbers: We converted text to mathematical vectors
Similarity Search: We used math to find the most relevant document
Context + LLM: In a real system, we’d feed this context to an LLM for a polished answer

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
From Graphs to Networks
At its core, a Deep Neural Network is a directed, weighted graph. The evolution of modern AI architectures is fundamentally rooted in graph theory, where we map information flow from one set of nodes to another.

Nodes (Vertices): Represent “Neurons,” holding mathematical values.
Edges: Represent “Weights,” determining the strength of the connection between neurons.
Layers: Collections of isolated nodes.

Input Layer: Receives the raw tensor data.
Hidden Layers: The computational engine.
Output Layer: Produces the final prediction/probability.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Forward Propagation
Information flows forward through the graph. For any single neuron in a hidden layer, its value is the weighted sum of all connected neurons from the previous layer, plus a bias term.
\[z = \sum (w_i \cdot x_i) + b\] \[a = f(z)\]
Where \(f(z)\) is the Activation Function, introducing the non-linearity required to learn complex patterns.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Why we need Non-Linearity
Without activation functions, a neural network with 100 hidden layers is mathematically identical to a standard Linear Regression model. Non-linear activations allow the network to warp and fold the high-dimensional space.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
section: Evaluating Activations: ReLU, ELU, and SiLU
text:
Evaluating Activations: ReLU, ELU, and SiLU
Choosing the right activation unit is critical for optimal gradient flow during backpropagation and maximizing metrics like FID scores in generative tasks.

ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): \(f(x) = \max(0, x)\).

Pros: Computationally cheap.
Cons: The “Dying ReLU” problem (gradients become zero for negative inputs).

ELU (Exponential Linear Unit): Smooths the negative values using an exponential curve rather than a hard zero.

Pros: Pushes mean activation closer to zero, speeding up learning and preventing dead neurons.

SiLU (Sigmoid Linear Unit / Swish): \(f(x) = x \cdot \sigma(x)\).

Pros: Self-gated and smooth everywhere. Highly effective in deep architectures, consistently outperforming standard ReLU in advanced implementations.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Loss Functions
The Loss Function calculates the difference between our network’s output and the ground truth.

Mean Squared Error (MSE): Used for Regression tasks. Penalizes large errors heavily.
Categorical Cross-Entropy: Used for Multi-class Classification. Measures the difference between two probability distributions. \[L = -\sum y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)\]

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Mitigating Overfitting
Deep networks have millions of parameters; they will easily memorize the training data if left unchecked.

Dropout: During training, we randomly “drop” (set to zero) a percentage of neurons in a layer. This forces the network to distribute learning and prevents it from relying on any single node.
Early Stopping: We monitor a validation dataset. If the validation loss stops improving (or starts increasing) while training loss goes down, we halt training immediately.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
The TensorFlow & Keras Ecosystem
We do not write backpropagation calculus by hand in production. We use TensorFlow, with Keras acting as a highly intuitive, high-level API for defining our graph topologies.
The standard Keras workflow: 1. Define the architecture (Sequential or Functional API). 2. compile() the model (Define Optimizer, Loss, Metrics). 3. fit() the model to the training data. 4. evaluate() on unseen test data.

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Building a Deep Network
Let’s build a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for a complex classification task, utilizing the advanced activation units we discussed.

# Note: Ensure TensorFlow is installed in your environment
# pip install tensorflow
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import make_circles
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense, Dropout
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import Adam

# 1. Generate Non-Linear Data (Concentric Circles)
X, y = make_circles(n_samples=1000, noise=0.1, factor=0.2, random_state=42)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

# 2. Define the Neural Network Architecture
model = Sequential([
    # Input layer + Hidden Layer 1 (Using ELU)
    Dense(32, input_shape=(2,), activation='elu'),
    Dropout(0.2),
    
    # Hidden Layer 2 (Using SiLU)
    Dense(32, activation='silu'),
    Dropout(0.2),
    
    # Output Layer (Sigmoid for binary classification)
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

# 3. Compile the Engine
model.compile(optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.01),
              loss='binary_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])

# 4. Train the Model (Silenced output for presentation)
history = model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=30, batch_size=32, 
                    validation_split=0.2, verbose=0)

# 5. Evaluate
test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test, verbose=0)
print(f"Deep Neural Network Accuracy: {test_acc * 100:.2f}%")

Deep Neural Network Accuracy: 100.00%

title: Module 3: Deep Learning Architectures
text:
Visualizing the Training Curve
Did the model overfit? Let’s check the loss curves.

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(history.history['loss'], label='Training Loss', color='#153263')
plt.plot(history.history['val_loss'], label='Validation Loss', color='#e63946')
plt.title('Model Loss Over Epochs')
plt.ylabel('Binary Cross-Entropy Loss')
plt.xlabel('Epoch')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.show()












[1] I. Goodfellow, Y. Bengio, and A. Courville, Deep learning. MIT Press, 2016. Available: https://www.deeplearningbook.org


[2] D. P. Kingma and J. Ba, “Adam: A method for stochastic optimization,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1412.6980, 2014.


[3] A. Vaswani et al., “Attention is all you need,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 30, 2017.


[4] T. Brown et al., “Language models are few-shot learners,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 33, pp. 1877–1901, 2020.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
The Machine Learning Lifecycle
Before we train algorithms, we must manage our data and code. The modern AI engineer relies heavily on version control and open-source data repositories.

Source: Fetching data via the Kaggle API.
Version: Tracking code changes using Git and GitHub.
Explore: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA).
Engineer: Feature scaling, encoding, and imputation.
Model: Training and tuning.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
GitHub Fundamentals
Git is the version control system; GitHub is the cloud hosting platform. In AI, we use it to track experiment iterations.

git clone: Download a repository to your local machine/Colab.
git add .: Stage your modified Jupyter notebooks.
git commit -m "added XGBoost model": Save a snapshot of your progress.
git push: Send the snapshot to your GitHub portfolio.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
Sourcing Data: The Kaggle API
Kaggle hosts millions of datasets. Instead of downloading CSVs manually, we automate it using the Kaggle API.
Typical Colab Setup:
# 1. Upload your kaggle.json API token
import os
os.environ['KAGGLE_CONFIG_DIR'] = "/content"

# 2. Download the dataset directly via CLI
!kaggle competitions download -c titanic

# 3. Unzip
!unzip titanic.zip

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
What is Supervised Learning?
In supervised learning, we provide the algorithm with both the features (X) and the ground-truth labels (y). The goal is to learn a mapping function \(f(x) \approx y\).

Regression: The target variable y is continuous (e.g., predicting house prices, temperature).
Classification: The target variable y is discrete/categorical (e.g., spam vs. not spam, identifying diseases).

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
Linear & Polynomial Regression
Linear Regression assumes a straight-line relationship between inputs and outputs. \[ \hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n \] Optimized using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) or Gradient Descent.
Polynomial Regression transforms the features into higher degrees to fit non-linear data, though the equation itself remains a linear combination of weights. \[ \hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3 \]

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
Logistic Regression
Despite its name, Logistic Regression is a classification algorithm. It takes a linear equation and passes it through a Sigmoid Activation Function to squash the output between 0 and 1, representing a probability.
\[\sigma(z)= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\] Where \(z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n\) is the linear combination of features. If \(\sigma(z) \geq 0.5\), we classify as Class 1; otherwise, Class 0.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
Tree-Based Architectures
Decision Trees split the data based on feature thresholds (e.g., “Is Age > 30?”). However, a single tree is highly prone to overfitting.
The Solution: Ensembles

Random Forest (Bagging): Trains hundreds of independent trees on random subsets of data and averages their predictions. Excellent out-of-the-box performance.
XGBoost (Boosting): Trains trees sequentially. Each new tree focuses specifically on correcting the errors (residuals) made by the previous trees. The current king of tabular data competitions.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
The Unsupervised Paradigm
In Unsupervised Learning, we only have features (X). There are no labels (y). The algorithm must discover the hidden structure, patterns, or groupings within the data on its own.
Primary use cases:

Clustering: Grouping similar data points together.
Dimensionality Reduction: Compressing features while retaining information.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
K-Means Clustering
The most popular clustering algorithm. It partitions data into \(K\) distinct clusters. The Algorithm:

Randomly place \(K\) centroids in the data space.
Assign: Assign each data point to its nearest centroid.
Update: Move the centroid to the mean position of all assigned points.
Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until the centroids stop moving (convergence).

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
Why Reduce Dimensions?
High-dimensional data (e.g., 1000+ features) suffers from the “Curse of Dimensionality.” Distance metrics lose meaning, and models overfit. We must compress it.

PCA (Principal Component Analysis): A linear technique. It finds the orthogonal axes (Eigenvectors) of maximum variance and projects the data onto them. Great for general compression.
t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding): A non-linear technique. It focuses on preserving local neighborhoods (keeping similar points close together). Exceptional for visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D or 3D.

title: Module 2: Classical Machine Learning
text:
End-to-End Scikit-Learn Pipeline
Let’s build a complete classification pipeline using a synthetic dataset. We will train a Random Forest and evaluate its decision boundary.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

# 1. Generate Non-Linear Data
X, y = make_moons(n_samples=300, noise=0.25, random_state=42)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3)

# 2. Initialize and Train the Model
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=5, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# 3. Evaluate
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
acc = accuracy_score(y_test, predictions)
print(f"Random Forest Accuracy: {acc * 100:.2f}%")

# 4. Visualize Decision Boundary
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-1.5, 2.5, 100), np.linspace(-1, 1.5, 100))
Z = model.predict(np.c_[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]).reshape(xx.shape)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.contourf(xx, yy, Z, alpha=0.3, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.scatter(X_test[:, 0], X_test[:, 1], c=y_test, edgecolor='k', cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title(f"Random Forest Decision Boundary (Acc: {acc*100:.0f}%)")
plt.show()

Random Forest Accuracy: 86.67%













[1] I. Goodfellow, Y. Bengio, and A. Courville, Deep learning. MIT Press, 2016. Available: https://www.deeplearningbook.org


[2] D. P. Kingma and J. Ba, “Adam: A method for stochastic optimization,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1412.6980, 2014.


[3] A. Vaswani et al., “Attention is all you need,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 30, 2017.


[4] T. Brown et al., “Language models are few-shot learners,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 33, pp. 1877–1901, 2020.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Vector Spaces & Tensors
In machine learning, data is represented numerically as arrays. These arrays generalize into tensors, residing within vector spaces.

Scalar (0D Tensor): A single magnitude.
Vector (1D Tensor): An array of numbers, representing a point in space \(v \in \mathbb{R}^n\).
Matrix (2D Tensor): A 2D grid, representing a collection of vectors or linear transformations.

The Machine Learning Connection: An image is often represented as a 3D tensor (Height \(\times\) Width \(\times\) Color Channels).

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Orthogonality & Matrices
Two vectors \(u\) and \(v\) are orthogonal if their dot product is zero: \[ u^T v = 0 \] Orthogonality is a critical concept in feature engineering—ideally, we want independent features that do not carry redundant information.
A matrix \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}\) acts as a linear transformation mapping a vector from \(\mathbb{R}^n\) to \(\mathbb{R}^m\). \[ y = Ax \]

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors
For a square matrix \(A\), an eigenvector \(v\) is a non-zero vector that changes at most by a scalar factor (the eigenvalue \(\lambda\)) when the linear transformation \(A\) is applied to it.
\[ Av = \lambda v \]
Why it matters: In AI, eigenvectors define the “principal axes” of our data sets, pointing in the direction of maximum variance.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
SVD generalizes eigendecomposition to non-square matrices and is the fundamental math behind Dimensionality Reduction (like PCA).
Any matrix \(A\) can be decomposed as: \[ A = U \Sigma V^T \]

\(U\): Orthogonal matrix containing left singular vectors.
\(\Sigma\): Diagonal matrix containing singular values (importance of each feature).
\(V^T\): Orthogonal matrix containing right singular vectors.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Partial Derivatives
In AI, we deal with functions of multiple variables (e.g., millions of neural network weights). The partial derivative measures how the function changes as we tweak just one variable, keeping others constant.
For a cost function \(J(\theta_1, \theta_2)\): \[ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} \]

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
The Chain Rule & Backpropagation
How do we find the derivative of a nested function? The Chain Rule. If \(y = f(u)\) and \(u = g(x)\), then: \[ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} \]
Intuition for AI: In deep neural networks, the error at the output layer is propagated backwards through the hidden layers by multiplying the local gradients (derivatives) at each layer using the chain rule.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Gradient Descent Mechanics
To train a model, we want to find the parameters \(\theta\) that minimize the cost function \(J(\theta)\). The gradient \(\nabla_\theta J(\theta)\) points in the direction of steepest ascent.
We update parameters by moving in the opposite direction: \[ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t) \] Where \(\alpha\) is the learning rate.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Advanced Optimization: SGD & Adam

Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): Instead of calculating the gradient using the entire dataset, SGD uses a single random data point (or a small batch) per step. It is faster but noisy.
Adam Optimizer: Computes adaptive learning rates for each parameter. It keeps an exponentially decaying average of past gradients (momentum) and past squared gradients.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Bayesian vs. Frequentist Probability

Frequentist: Probability is the long-run frequency of repeatable events. Parameters are fixed, and data is a repeatable random sample.
Bayesian: Probability is a degree of belief. Parameters are treated as random variables with a prior distribution, updated as new data is observed.

Bayes’ Theorem: \[ P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)} \]

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Gaussian Distributions & Hypothesis Testing
The Normal (Gaussian) distribution is ubiquitous in AI due to the Central Limit Theorem. \[ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2} \]
Hypothesis Testing in AI: Used for model evaluation. Are the predictions of Model A statistically significantly better than Model B, or is the difference due to random noise? We use p-values to evaluate the null hypothesis.

title: Module 1: Mathematical Foundations for AI
text:
Coding Gradient Descent from Scratch
Let’s minimize a simple cost function \(J(\theta) = \theta^2 + 5\) using Python.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Objective function and its derivative
def cost_function(theta):
    return theta**2 + 5

def gradient(theta):
    return 2 * theta

# Gradient Descent Parameters
theta = 8.0  # Initial guess
learning_rate = 0.1
iterations = 15
history = []

for _ in range(iterations):
    history.append((theta, cost_function(theta)))
    theta = theta - learning_rate * gradient(theta)

# Visualization
theta_vals = np.linspace(-10, 10, 100)
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(theta_vals, cost_function(theta_vals), label=r'$J(\theta)$', zorder=1)

# Plotting the steps
history = np.array(history)
plt.scatter(history[:, 0], history[:, 1], color='red', zorder=2, label='GD Steps')
plt.plot(history[:, 0], history[:, 1], color='red', linestyle='--', zorder=2)

plt.title('Gradient Descent Optimization')
plt.xlabel(r'$\theta$')
plt.ylabel(r'$J(\theta)$')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()







Figure 1: Visualizing the optimization path of Gradient Descent









[1] I. Goodfellow, Y. Bengio, and A. Courville, Deep learning. MIT Press, 2016. Available: https://www.deeplearningbook.org


[2] D. P. Kingma and J. Ba, “Adam: A method for stochastic optimization,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1412.6980, 2014.


[3] A. Vaswani et al., “Attention is all you need,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 30, 2017.


[4] T. Brown et al., “Language models are few-shot learners,” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 33, pp. 1877–1901, 2020.

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

Visualize matrix operations as transformations of space rather than mere calculations
Compute and interpret eigenvalues and eigenvectors in the context of data analysis
Implement gradient descent from scratch and understand its role in training neural networks
Apply Bayes’ theorem to update beliefs based on new evidence
Use Gaussian distributions to model uncertainty in machine learning predictions
Perform hypothesis testing to statistically compare model performances
Translate mathematical concepts into working Python code using NumPy and Matplotlib

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
section: 1.1 Don’t Just Calculate: See the Matrix
text:
1.1 Don’t Just Calculate: See the Matrix
Most of us were taught matrix multiplication using the “row picture”—taking the dot product of rows from the first matrix and columns from the second. It works, but it hides what is actually happening.
In machine learning, we need to shift to the Column Picture.

The Row Picture (Traditional Approach)
Given: \[
A \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
\]
The traditional method computes: \[
\begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 + (-1)x_2 \\ 1x_1 + 1x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 - x_2 \\ x_1 + x_2 \end{bmatrix}
\]


The Column Picture (AI Perspective)
Instead of computing the dots, think of \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) as weights. The matrix multiplication is simply a linear combination of the columns of \(A\):
\[
x_1 \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + x_2 \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\]






Why the Column Picture Matters



The column picture reveals that \(A\mathbf{x}\) is a linear combination of the columns of \(A\), with the entries of \(\mathbf{x}\) as weights. This perspective is fundamental because:

The column space of \(A\) (all possible linear combinations of columns) tells us what outputs are achievable
In neural networks, each layer transforms input data into a new space spanned by its weight matrix columns
Understanding this helps visualize how data is “remixed” at each layer of a deep network





Why is This Profound for AI?
Because every time a neural network layer processes data, it is taking your input vector and projecting it into a new space defined by the columns of the weight matrix. The matrix is a living, breathing transformation.

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
1.2 Visualizing the Transformation
Let’s use Python to see what the matrix \(A\) actually does to a set of points in 2D space.


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create a grid of points (our original space)
x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 10)
y = np.linspace(-5, 5, 10)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
points = np.vstack([X.flatten(), Y.flatten()])

# Our Transformation Matrix A
A = np.array([[2, -1], 
              [1,  1]])

# Apply the transformation
transformed_points = A @ points

# Plotting
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

ax1.scatter(points[0], points[1], c='blue', s=15, alpha=0.7)
ax1.set_title("Original Space", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax1.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax1.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax1.set_xlim(-6, 6)
ax1.set_ylim(-6, 6)
ax1.set_aspect('equal')
ax1.set_xlabel('$x_1$')
ax1.set_ylabel('$x_2$')

ax2.scatter(transformed_points[0], transformed_points[1], c='red', s=15, alpha=0.7)
ax2.set_title("Space after Transformation by A", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax2.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax2.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax2.set_xlim(-15, 15)
ax2.set_ylim(-15, 15)
ax2.set_aspect('equal')
ax2.set_xlabel("$x'_1$")
ax2.set_ylabel("$x'_2$")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()















Key Insight



The matrix \(A\) is not just a set of numbers; it is a function that takes every point in the original space and moves it to a new location. This is the essence of how data flows through neural networks—each layer applies a transformation that reshapes the data into a form that makes it easier for the model to learn patterns.
Notice how the grid is sheared and rotated. The matrix has stretched the space. In Deep Learning, we use these transformations (followed by non-linear activations) to warp our data until complex patterns become linearly separable [1].

text:

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
2.1 Understanding Eigenvectors
If matrices are transformations that push and pull space, what happens to the vectors inside that space? Most vectors get knocked off their original span. They change direction.
But for a given square matrix, there are a few special vectors that are completely immune to being knocked off course. The matrix might stretch them or shrink them, but it cannot change their direction. These stubborn vectors are the Eigenvectors.

Mathematical Definition
For a square matrix \(A\), an eigenvector \(\mathbf{v}\) and its corresponding eigenvalue \(\lambda\) satisfy:
\[ A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v} \]
This means that when you apply the transformation \(A\) to \(\mathbf{v}\), it only gets scaled by \(\lambda\) but does not change direction.


Visual Demonstration


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define a matrix A
A = np.array([[2, -1], 
              [1,  1]])

# Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(A)

# Create a grid of points
x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 15)
y = np.linspace(-5, 5, 15)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
points = np.vstack([X.flatten(), Y.flatten()])

# Transform the points
transformed_points = A @ points

# Plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))

# Plot original grid (light)
ax.scatter(points[0], points[1], c='lightgray', s=5, alpha=0.5, label='Original grid')

# Plot transformed grid
ax.scatter(transformed_points[0], transformed_points[1], c='gray', s=5, alpha=0.3)

# Plot eigenvectors (scaled for visibility)
colors = ['red', 'blue']
for i in range(len(eigenvalues)):
    v = eigenvectors[:, i]
    # Scale eigenvector for visualization
    scale = 5
    ax.quiver(0, 0, v[0]*scale, v[1]*scale, 
              angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, 
              color=colors[i], width=0.008, 
              label=f'v{i+1}: λ={eigenvalues[i]:.3f}')
    # Plot the transformed eigenvector
    Av = A @ v
    ax.quiver(0, 0, Av[0]*scale, Av[1]*scale, 
              angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, 
              color=colors[i], width=0.008, alpha=0.5, linestyle='--')

ax.set_xlim(-15, 15)
ax.set_ylim(-15, 15)
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_title('Eigenvectors of Matrix A', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
plt.show()

print("Eigenvalues:", eigenvalues)
print("\nEigenvectors (columns):")
print(eigenvectors)

# Verify: Av = λv
print("\nVerification (Av = λv):")
for i in range(len(eigenvalues)):
    v = eigenvectors[:, i]
    lam = eigenvalues[i]
    Av = A @ v
    lam_v = lam * v
    print(f"\nEigenvector {i+1}:")
    print(f"  A·v = {Av}")
    print(f"  λ·v = {lam_v}")
    print(f"  Match: {np.allclose(Av, lam_v)}")









Eigenvalues: [1.5+0.8660254j 1.5-0.8660254j]

Eigenvectors (columns):
[[0.35355339+0.61237244j 0.35355339-0.61237244j]
 [0.70710678+0.j         0.70710678-0.j        ]]

Verification (Av = λv):

Eigenvector 1:
  A·v = [2.22044605e-16+1.22474487j 1.06066017e+00+0.61237244j]
  λ·v = [3.60822483e-16+1.22474487j 1.06066017e+00+0.61237244j]
  Match: True

Eigenvector 2:
  A·v = [2.22044605e-16-1.22474487j 1.06066017e+00-0.61237244j]
  λ·v = [3.60822483e-16-1.22474487j 1.06066017e+00-0.61237244j]
  Match: True

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
section: 2.2 Computing Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors in Python
text:
2.2 Computing Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors in Python


Show Code
import numpy as np

# Define a symmetric matrix (common in ML - e.g., covariance matrices)
A = np.array([[4, 2],
              [2, 3]])

print("Matrix A:")
print(A)

# Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(A)

print(f"\nEigenvalues: {eigenvalues}")
print(f"\nEigenvectors (each column is an eigenvector):")
print(eigenvectors)

# Verify the eigendecomposition
print("\nVerification:")
for i in range(len(eigenvalues)):
    v = eigenvectors[:, i]
    lam = eigenvalues[i]
    residual = np.linalg.norm(A @ v - lam * v)
    print(f"  ||A·v{i+1} - λ{i+1}·v{i+1}|| = {residual:.2e}")

# Reconstruct the matrix using eigendecomposition
# A = V Λ V^(-1)
Lambda = np.diag(eigenvalues)
V = eigenvectors
A_reconstructed = V @ Lambda @ np.linalg.inv(V)

print(f"\nReconstructed A:")
print(A_reconstructed)
print(f"\nReconstruction error: {np.linalg.norm(A - A_reconstructed):.2e}")


Matrix A:
[[4 2]
 [2 3]]

Eigenvalues: [5.56155281 1.43844719]

Eigenvectors (each column is an eigenvector):
[[ 0.78820544 -0.61541221]
 [ 0.61541221  0.78820544]]

Verification:
  ||A·v1 - λ1·v1|| = 4.44e-16
  ||A·v2 - λ2·v2|| = 1.11e-16

Reconstructed A:
[[4. 2.]
 [2. 3.]]

Reconstruction error: 6.66e-16

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
section: 2.3 Why Do Eigenvectors Matter in AI?
text:
2.3 Why Do Eigenvectors Matter in AI?

Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
In machine learning, especially in dimensionality reduction techniques like PCA, we are looking for the directions in which our data varies the most. These directions are precisely the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix of the data. The eigenvalues tell us how much variance is captured along each eigenvector.


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Generate correlated 2D data
np.random.seed(42)
mean = [0, 0]
cov = [[3, 2], [2, 2]]  # Covariance matrix
data = np.random.multivariate_normal(mean, cov, 200)

# Standardize the data
scaler = StandardScaler()
data_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(data)

# Compute covariance matrix
cov_matrix = np.cov(data_scaled.T)
print("Covariance Matrix:")
print(cov_matrix)

# Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of covariance matrix
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(cov_matrix)

print(f"\nEigenvalues (variance explained): {eigenvalues}")
print(f"Proportion of variance: {eigenvalues / eigenvalues.sum()}")

# Apply PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
pca.fit(data_scaled)

print(f"\nPCA Components (should match eigenvectors):")
print(pca.components_)
print(f"\nExplained variance: {pca.explained_variance_}")

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(data_scaled[:, 0], data_scaled[:, 1], alpha=0.6, label='Data points')

# Plot eigenvectors (scaled)
for i in range(len(eigenvalues)):
    v = eigenvectors[:, i] * np.sqrt(eigenvalues[i]) * 2
    ax.quiver(0, 0, v[0], v[1], angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1,
              color='red', width=0.005, label=f'PC{i+1} ({eigenvalues[i]/eigenvalues.sum()*100:.1f}%)')

ax.set_xlabel('Feature 1 (standardized)')
ax.set_ylabel('Feature 2 (standardized)')
ax.set_title('PCA: Eigenvectors Point in Directions of Maximum Variance')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_aspect('equal')
plt.show()


Covariance Matrix:
[[1.00502513 0.81957449]
 [0.81957449 1.00502513]]

Eigenvalues (variance explained): [1.82459962 0.18545063]
Proportion of variance: [0.90773831 0.09226169]

PCA Components (should match eigenvectors):
[[ 0.70710678  0.70710678]
 [ 0.70710678 -0.70710678]]

Explained variance: [1.82459962 0.18545063]











Application: Face Recognition with Eigenfaces
Eigenvectors are used in Eigenfaces for face recognition, where the eigenvectors of a set of face images form a basis for “face space” [2].

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
3.1 The Optimization Problem
We have a neural network, and it is making terrible predictions. We measure how terrible it is using a Cost Function, \(J(\theta)\). We want to find the parameters \(\theta\) that make \(J(\theta)\) as close to zero as possible.
Imagine you are blindfolded, standing on a rugged, mountainous landscape (the Cost Function). You want to reach the bottom of the valley. How do you do it?
You feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. You calculate the derivative.

If the slope is positive (going uphill), you take a step backwards.
If the slope is negative (going downhill), you take a step forwards.

In multiple dimensions, this “slope” is the Gradient, \(\nabla J\). It is a vector that always points in the direction of steepest ascent.
To minimize our error, we simply walk in the opposite direction of the gradient. This is the update rule of Gradient Descent:
\[ \theta = \theta - \alpha \nabla J(\theta) \]
Where \(\alpha\) is the learning rate, controlling how big our steps are. Too big, and we might overshoot the minimum. Too small, and we might take forever to get there.

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
section: 3.2 Building the Engine: Gradient Descent from Scratch
text:
3.2 Building the Engine: Gradient Descent from Scratch
Let’s build the engine of optimization. We won’t use libraries for this; we will write the raw math in Python.

Example 1: Simple 1D Gradient Descent


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1. Our objective function: J(theta) = theta^2 + 5
def J(theta):
    return theta**2 + 5

# 2. The derivative: dJ/dtheta = 2*theta
def gradient(theta):
    return 2 * theta

# 3. The Optimization Loop
theta = 10.0      # Start at a random high point on the mountain
alpha = 0.1       # Step size (Learning rate)
epochs = 15       # How many steps we take

# Store history for plotting
theta_history = [theta]
cost_history = [J(theta)]

print(f"Starting Theta: {theta:.4f}, Initial Cost: {J(theta):.4f}")
print("-" * 50)

for i in range(epochs):
    grad = gradient(theta)
    theta = theta - (alpha * grad)  # The update rule
    
    theta_history.append(theta)
    cost_history.append(J(theta))
    
    if i % 3 == 0:
        print(f"Step {i+1:>2}: Theta = {theta:.4f}, Cost = {J(theta):.4f}, Gradient = {grad:.4f}")

print("-" * 50)
print(f"Final Optimized Theta: {theta:.4f}")
print(f"Final Cost: {J(theta):.4f}")

# Visualization
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Plot 1: Cost function with path
theta_vals = np.linspace(-12, 12, 200)
ax1.plot(theta_vals, J(theta_vals), 'b-', linewidth=2, label=r'$J(\theta) = \theta^2 + 5$')
ax1.scatter(theta_history, cost_history, c='red', s=50, zorder=5, label='Gradient Descent Path')
ax1.plot(theta_history, cost_history, 'r--', alpha=0.5)
ax1.set_xlabel(r'$\theta$', fontsize=12)
ax1.set_ylabel(r'$J(\theta)$', fontsize=12)
ax1.set_title('Cost Function and GD Path', fontsize=14)
ax1.legend()
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Plot 2: Cost over iterations
ax2.plot(range(len(cost_history)), cost_history, 'g-', linewidth=2)
ax2.set_xlabel('Iteration', fontsize=12)
ax2.set_ylabel('Cost', fontsize=12)

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
3.3 Gradient Descent Variants

Batch vs. Stochastic Gradient Descent


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Generate some data for linear regression
np.random.seed(42)
X = 2 * np.random.randn(100, 1)
y = 4 + 3 * X + np.random.randn(100, 1)

# Add bias term
X_b = np.c_[np.ones((100, 1)), X]

# Cost function for linear regression
def compute_cost(X, y, theta):
    m = len(y)
    return (1/(2*m)) * np.sum((X @ theta - y)**2)

def compute_gradient(X, y, theta):
    m = len(y)
    return (1/m) * X.T @ (X @ theta - y)

# Batch Gradient Descent
def batch_gd(X, y, theta, alpha, epochs):
    cost_history = []
    for _ in range(epochs):
        grad = compute_gradient(X, y, theta)
        theta = theta - alpha * grad
        cost_history.append(compute_cost(X, y, theta))
    return theta, cost_history

# Stochastic Gradient Descent
def sgd(X, y, theta, alpha, epochs):
    cost_history = []
    m = len(y)
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        # Shuffle data
        indices = np.random.permutation(m)
        X_shuffled = X[indices]
        y_shuffled = y[indices]
        for i in range(m):
            xi = X_shuffled[i:i+1]
            yi = y_shuffled[i:i+1]
            grad = (xi.T @ (xi @ theta - yi))  # Gradient for single sample
            theta = theta - alpha * grad
        cost_history.append(compute_cost(X, y, theta))
    return theta, cost_history

# Mini-batch Gradient Descent
def mini_batch_gd(X, y, theta, alpha, epochs, batch_size=10):
    cost_history = []
    m = len(y)
    for epoch in range(epochs):
        indices = np.random.permutation(m)
        X_shuffled = X[indices]
        y_shuffled = y[indices]
        for i in range(0, m, batch_size):
            xi = X_shuffled[i:i+batch_size]
            yi = y_shuffled[i:i+batch_size]
            grad = (1/batch_size) * xi.T @ (xi @ theta - yi)
            theta = theta - alpha * grad
        cost_history.append(compute_cost(X, y, theta))
    return theta, cost_history

# Run all three
theta_init = np.random.randn(2, 1)
alpha = 0.1
epochs = 50

theta_batch, cost_batch = batch_gd(X_b, y, theta_init.copy(), alpha, epochs)
theta_sgd, cost_sgd = sgd(X_b, y, theta_init.copy(), alpha, epochs)
theta_mini, cost_mini = mini_batch_gd(X_b, y, theta_init.copy(), alpha, epochs, batch_size=10)

# Plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

axes[0].plot(range(len(cost_batch)), cost_batch, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='Batch GD')
axes[0].plot(range(len(cost_sgd)), cost_sgd, 'r-', linewidth=1, alpha=0.7, label='SGD')
axes[0].plot(range(len(cost_mini)), cost_mini, 'g-', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8, label='Mini-batch GD')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Epoch')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Cost')
axes[0].set_title('Cost Convergence Comparison')
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

axes[1].semilogy(range(len(cost_batch)), cost_batch, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='Batch GD')
axes[1].semilogy(range(len(cost_sgd)), cost_sgd, 'r-', linewidth=1, alpha=0.7, label='SGD')
axes[1].semilogy(range(len(cost_mini)), cost_mini, 'g-', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8, label='Mini-batch GD')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Epoch')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Cost (log scale)')
axes[1].set_title('Log Scale Comparison')
axes[1].legend()
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"SGD final theta: {theta_sgd.flatten()}")

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
4.1 The Two Philosophies
In calculus and linear algebra, we deal with certainties. A matrix transforms a vector to one specific place. A gradient points in one specific direction. But the real world is messy, and data is noisy.
Machine learning is fundamentally about making optimal decisions under uncertainty. To do that, we need a mathematical framework for the unknown: Probability.

Frequentist vs. Bayesian
Before we look at equations, we must understand the two radically different ways mathematicians view probability:

The Frequentist View (The Casino): Probability is the long-run frequency of repeatable events. If you flip a coin 10,000 times, it will land on heads 50% of the time. In this view, parameters are fixed realities, and the data we collect is just a random sample.
The Bayesian View (The Detective): Probability is a degree of belief. It quantifies our uncertainty about a proposition. In this view, our parameters are uncertain (random variables), and the data we observe is fixed.

Modern AI, especially Generative AI, leans heavily on the Bayesian view. We start with a prior belief, observe some data, and update our belief [3].

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
section: 4.2 Bayes’ Theorem: The Engine of Updating
text:
4.2 Bayes’ Theorem: The Engine of Updating
Bayes’ theorem is not just a formula; it is a logical framework for changing your mind when presented with new evidence.
\[P(\text{Hypothesis} | \text{Data}) = \frac{P(\text{Data} | \text{Hypothesis}) \cdot P(\text{Hypothesis})}{P(\text{Data})}\]
Let’s translate this into the language of Machine Learning:







Term
ML Interpretation




\(P(\text{Hypothesis})\)
Prior: What we believed before seeing any data


\(P(\text{Data} | \text{Hypothesis})\)
Likelihood: How probable is the data we’re looking at, assuming our hypothesis is true?


\(P(\text{Hypothesis} | \text{Data})\)
Posterior: Our newly updated belief. This is what we want to find!


\(P(\text{Data})\)
Evidence: Normalizing constant



When you train a neural network, you are essentially trying to find the parameters (Hypothesis) that maximize this Posterior probability given the training images (Data).

Example: Medical Testing


Show Code
import numpy as np

# Medical testing example
# Disease prevalence: 1% of population
P_disease = 0.01

# Test sensitivity: P(Positive | Disease) = 99%
P_positive_given_disease = 0.99

# Test specificity: P(Negative | No Disease) = 95%
P_negative_given_no_disease = 0.95
P_positive_given_no_disease = 1 - P_negative_given_no_disease

# Question: If you test positive, what's the probability you actually have the disease?
# P(Disease | Positive) = ?

# Using Bayes' theorem:
# P(Disease | Positive) = P(Positive | Disease) * P(Disease) / P(Positive)

# P(Positive) = P(Positive | Disease) * P(Disease) + P(Positive | No Disease) * P(No Disease)
P_positive = (P_positive_given_disease * P_disease + 
              P_positive_given_no_disease * (1 - P_disease))

P_disease_given_positive = (P_positive_given_disease * P_disease) / P_positive

print("Medical Testing Example")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Disease prevalence: {P_disease*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Test sensitivity: {P_positive_given_disease*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Test specificity: {P_negative_given_no_disease*100:.1f}%")
print()
print(f"P(Positive test) = {P_positive:.4f}")
print(f"P(Disease | Positive test) = {P_disease_given_positive:.4f}")
print(f"P(Disease | Positive test) = {P_disease_given_positive*100:.2f}%")
print()
print("Surprising result! Even with a 99% accurate test,")
print(f"a positive result only means {P_disease_given_positive*100:.1f}% chance of disease.")
print("This is because the disease is rare (base rate fallacy).")


Medical Testing Example
==================================================
Disease prevalence: 1.0%
Test sensitivity: 99.0%
Test specificity: 95.0%

P(Positive test) = 0.0594
P(Disease | Positive test) = 0.1667
P(Disease | Positive test) = 16.67%

Surprising result! Even with a 99% accurate test,
a positive result only means 16.7% chance of disease.
This is because the disease is rare (base rate fallacy).




Visualizing Bayes’ Theorem


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import beta

# Prior: We believe the coin is fair (Beta distribution)
alpha_prior = 2  # "pseudo-heads"
beta_prior = 2   # "pseudo-tails"

# Simulate coin flips
np.random.seed(42)
true_bias = 0.7  # The coin is actually biased towards heads
flips = np.random.random(20) < true_bias

# Update posterior after each flip
posteriors = []
x = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))
axes = axes.flatten()

# Plot prior
ax = axes[0]
prior = beta.pdf(x, alpha_prior, beta_prior)
ax.plot(x, prior, 'b-', linewidth=2, label='Prior')
ax.fill_between(x, prior, alpha=0.3, color='blue')
ax.set_title(f'Prior: Beta({alpha_prior}, {beta_prior})')
ax.set_xlabel('θ (probability of heads)')
ax.set_ylabel('Density')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Update and plot after selected numbers of flips
update_points = [1, 5, 15]
for i, n_updates in enumerate(update_points):
    ax = axes[i + 1]
    
    # Update parameters
    alpha_post = alpha_prior + sum(flips[:n_updates])
    beta_post = beta_prior + n_updates - sum(flips[:n_updates])
    
    posterior = beta.pdf(x, alpha_post, beta_post)
    ax.plot(x, posterior, 'r-', linewidth=2, label=f'Posterior')
    ax.fill_between(x, posterior, alpha=0.3, color='red')
    ax.axvline(true_bias, color='green', linestyle='--', label=f'True θ = {true_bias}')
    ax.axvline(alpha_post/(alpha_post+beta_post), color='orange', linestyle=':', 
               label=f'MAP = {alpha_post/(alpha_post+beta_post):.2f}')
    
    heads = sum(flips[:n_updates])
    ax.set_title(f'After {n_updates} flips: {heads}H, {n_updates-heads}T\nPosterior: Beta({alpha_post}, {beta_post})')
    ax.set_xlabel('θ (probability of heads)')
    ax.set_ylabel('Density')
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

section: 4.3 The Shape of Nature: Gaussian Distributions
text:
4.3 The Shape of Nature: Gaussian Distributions
Why do we see the bell curve everywhere in statistics? Because of the Central Limit Theorem. It states that when you add up many independent random variables, their normalized sum tends toward a Gaussian distribution, regardless of their original distributions.
Since noise in the real world (sensor errors, human variations, wind currents) is usually the sum of many small, random factors, that noise is almost always Gaussian.
The Gaussian distribution is completely defined by just two parameters: - Mean (\(\mu\)): Where is the center of the data? - Variance (\(\sigma^2\)): How spread out is the data?
\[f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}\]

Visualizing Gaussian Distributions


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm

x = np.linspace(-10, 10, 1000)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Effect of different means (same variance)
ax1 = axes[0]
for mu in [-2, 0, 3]:
    y = norm.pdf(x, mu, 1)
    ax1.plot(x, y, linewidth=2, label=f'μ = {mu}, σ = 1')
ax1.set_title('Effect of Mean (μ) on Gaussian', fontsize=14)
ax1.set_xlabel('x')
ax1.set_ylabel('Probability Density')
ax1.legend()
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Effect of different variances (same mean)
ax2 = axes[1]
for sigma in [0.5, 1, 2]:
    y = norm.pdf(x, 0, sigma)
    ax2.plot(x, y, linewidth=2, label=f'μ = 0, σ = {sigma}')
ax2.set_title('Effect of Variance (σ) on Gaussian', fontsize=14)
ax2.set_xlabel('x')
ax2.set_ylabel('Probability Density')
ax2.legend()
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











The 68-95-99.7 Rule


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mu, sigma = 0, 1

title: Module 1: Deep Dive Notes
text:
4.4 Hypothesis Testing in AI

Did We Actually Build a Better Model?
Suppose you train a standard Logistic Regression model (Model A) and it gets 85% accuracy. Then, you build a complex Deep Learning model (Model B) and it gets 87% accuracy.
Is Model B actually better? Or did it just get lucky with the specific testing data you used?
This is where Hypothesis Testing comes in.

The Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\)): Model B is not better. Any difference in accuracy is just random noise.
The p-value: The probability of seeing a 2% improvement if the Null Hypothesis were true.

If the p-value is extremely low (typically \(< 0.05\)), we reject the Null Hypothesis and confidently say, “Model B is genuinely superior.”


Comparing Model Performances


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm, ttest_ind

# Simulated cross-validation accuracy data
np.random.seed(42)

# Model A: 10-fold cross-validation accuracies
acc_A = np.array([0.84, 0.86, 0.83, 0.85, 0.87, 0.84, 0.85, 0.86, 0.84, 0.85])

# Model B: 10-fold cross-validation accuracies
acc_B = np.array([0.87, 0.88, 0.85, 0.89, 0.86, 0.88, 0.87, 0.86, 0.88, 0.87])

mean_A, std_A = np.mean(acc_A), np.std(acc_A)
mean_B, std_B = np.mean(acc_B), np.std(acc_B)

print("Model Comparison Statistics")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Model A: Mean = {mean_A:.4f}, Std = {std_A:.4f}")
print(f"Model B: Mean = {mean_B:.4f}, Std = {std_B:.4f}")
print(f"Difference: {mean_B - mean_A:.4f}")

# Perform t-test
t_stat, p_value = ttest_ind(acc_A, acc_B, alternative='less')  # One-tailed: is B > A?
print(f"\nt-statistic: {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value: {p_value:.6f}")

if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f"\n✓ Statistically significant! (p < 0.05)")
    print("Model B is significantly better than Model A.")
else:
    print(f"\n✗ Not statistically significant (p >= 0.05)")
    print("Cannot conclude Model B is better.")

# Visualization
x = np.linspace(0.75, 0.95, 200)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

y_A = norm.pdf(x, mean_A, std_A)
y_B = norm.pdf(x, mean_B, std_B)

ax.plot(x, y_A, color='#153263', linewidth=2, label=f'Model A (μ={mean_A:.3f})')
ax.fill_between(x, y_A, alpha=0.3, color='#153263')

ax.plot(x, y_B, color='#e63946', linewidth=2, label=f'Model B (μ={mean_B:.3f})')
ax.fill_between(x, y_B, alpha=0.3, color='#e63946')

ax.axvline(mean_A, color='#153263', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
ax.axvline(mean_B, color='#e63946', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Add individual CV scores
ax.scatter(acc_A, np.zeros_like(acc_A) + 0.02, color='#153263', s=50, zorder=5, marker='|')
ax.scatter(acc_B, np.zeros_like(acc_B) + 0.04, color='#e63946', s=50, zorder=5, marker='|')

ax.set_title(f'Model Comparison (p-value = {p_value:.6f})', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Accuracy')
ax.set_ylabel('Probability Density')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

Model B: Mean = 0.8710, Std = 0.0114
Difference: 0.0220

Solution 3.2: Gradient Descent with Momentum


Show Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def f(x):
    return x**2

def grad_f(x):
    return 2*x

# Standard GD
def standard_gd(x0, alpha, epochs):
    x = x0
    history = [x]
    for _ in range(epochs):
        x = x - alpha * grad_f(x)
        history.append(x)
    return history

# GD with momentum
def momentum_gd(x0, alpha, beta, epochs):
    x = x0
    v = 0
    history = [x]
    for _ in range(epochs):
        v = beta * v + alpha * grad_f(x)
        x = x - v
        history.append(x)
    return history

# Compare
x0 = 10
alpha = 0.1
epochs = 20

std_history = standard_gd(x0, alpha, epochs)
moments = [0, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot(range(len(std_history)), std_history, 'b-', label='Standard GD', linewidth=2)

for beta in moments:
    mom_history = momentum_gd(x0, alpha, beta, epochs)
    plt.plot(range(len(mom_history)), mom_history, '--', label=f'Momentum β={beta}', alpha=0.7)

plt.xlabel('Iteration')
plt.ylabel('x')
plt.title('Gradient Descent with Momentum')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
In traditional programming, we explicitly write rules (the code), provide input data, and receive output answers:
\[\text{Rules} + \text{Data} \rightarrow \text{Answers}\]
Machine Learning fundamentally inverts this paradigm. We provide the data and the corresponding answers (labels), and the algorithm discovers the rules:
\[\text{Data} + \text{Answers} \rightarrow \text{Rules}\]



Supervised machine learning is fundamentally an exercise in function approximation. We posit the existence of an unknown true function \(f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}\) that maps input features to output labels:
\[y = f(x) + \epsilon\]
where \(\epsilon\) represents irreducible noise in the data.
Our objective is to construct an approximating function \(\hat{f}(x; \theta)\) parameterized by \(\theta\), such that \(\hat{f}\) minimizes the expected loss:
\[\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}}[\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{f}(x; \theta))]\]
where \(\mathcal{D}\) is the underlying data distribution and \(\mathcal{L}\) is a loss function.


The expected prediction error can be decomposed into three components:
\[\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}\]

Bias: Error from overly simplistic assumptions. High bias leads to underfitting.
Variance: Error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in training data. High variance leads to overfitting.
Irreducible Error: Noise inherent in the problem that cannot be reduced.

The goal of model selection is to find the optimal balance between bias and variance.

title: Module 2: Deep Dive into Classical Machine Learning
text:
Linear Regression: Theory and Derivation

The Model
Given a dataset \(\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^n\) where \(x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d\) and \(y^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}\), linear regression assumes:
\[\hat{y}^{(i)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \beta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \cdots + \beta_d x_d^{(i)} = \beta^T x^{(i)}\]
In matrix notation, with \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (d+1)}\) (including intercept column) and \(\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}\):
\[\hat{y} = X\beta\]


Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Solution
The OLS method minimizes the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS):
\[RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2 = (y - X\beta)^T(y - X\beta)\]
Taking the gradient with respect to \(\beta\) and setting it to zero:
\[\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta} = -2X^T(y - X\beta) = 0\]
Solving for \(\beta\) yields the Normal Equation:
\[\beta^* = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty\]


Probabilistic Interpretation
Assuming the errors are independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) following a Gaussian distribution:
\[\epsilon^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\]
The likelihood function becomes:
\[L(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\left(-\frac{(y^{(i)} - \beta^T x^{(i)})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\]
Maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing RSS, establishing that OLS is the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) under Gaussian noise assumptions.


Regularization: Ridge and Lasso
To prevent overfitting, we add penalty terms:
Ridge Regression (L2 Regularization): \[\beta^* = \arg\min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|^2_2 + \lambda \|\beta\|^2_2 \right\}\]
Solution: \(\beta^* = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty\)
Lasso Regression (L1 Regularization): \[\beta^* = \arg\min_{\beta} \left\{ \|y - X\beta\|^2_2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_1 \right\}\]
Lasso has the desirable property of inducing sparsity — it can shrink coefficients exactly to zero, performing automatic feature selection.


Show the Python Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression, Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

# Generate synthetic data
np.random.seed(42)
n_samples = 100
X = np.sort(np.random.uniform(0, 10, n_samples)).reshape(-1, 1)
y = 2.5 * X.squeeze() + 10 + np.random.normal(0, 5, n_samples)

# Fit Linear Regression
lr = LinearRegression()
lr.fit(X, y)

# Generate predictions
X_test = np.linspace(0, 10, 200).reshape(-1, 1)
y_pred = lr.predict(X_test)

# Calculate confidence intervals
n_bootstraps = 1000
bootstrap_preds = []
for _ in range(n_bootstraps):
    indices = np.random.choice(n_samples, n_samples, replace=True)
    lr_boot = LinearRegression()
    lr_boot.fit(X[indices], y[indices])
    bootstrap_preds.append(lr_boot.predict(X_test))

bootstrap_preds = np.array(bootstrap_preds)
lower_bound = np.percentile(bootstrap_preds, 2.5, axis=0)
upper_bound = np.percentile(bootstrap_preds, 97.5, axis=0)

# Plotting
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.scatter(X, y, alpha=0.6, label='Data Points', color='steelblue')
plt.plot(X_test, y_pred, 'r-', linewidth=2, label=f'OLS: y = {lr.coef_[0]:.2f}x + {lr.intercept_:.2f}')
plt.fill_between(X_test.squeeze(), lower_bound, upper_bound, alpha=0.2, color='red', label='95% Confidence Interval')
plt.xlabel('Feature X')
plt.ylabel('Target y')
plt.title('Linear Regression with Bootstrap Confidence Intervals')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

print(f"R² Score: {lr.score(X, y):.4f}")
print(f"Coefficients: {lr.coef_}")
print(f"Intercept: {lr.intercept_:.2f}")









R² Score: 0.7476
Coefficients: [2.63135488]
Intercept: 9.38




Polynomial Regression
When relationships are non-linear, we can transform features using polynomial basis functions:
\[\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \beta_3 x^3 + \cdots + \beta_p x^p\]
This remains a linear model because it’s linear in the parameters \(\beta\).


Show the Python Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# Generate non-linear data
np.random.seed(42)
n_samples = 50
X = np.sort(np.random.uniform(0, 4, n_samples)).reshape(-1, 1)
y = np.sin(X.squeeze()) + 0.1 * np.random.randn(n_samples)

X_test = np.linspace(0, 4, 200).reshape(-1, 1)

degrees = [1, 3, 5, 10]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
axes = axes.ravel()

for idx, degree in enumerate(degrees):
    # Create polynomial features
    poly_features = PolynomialFeatures(degree=degree)
    X_poly = poly_features.fit_transform(X)
    X_test_poly = poly_features.transform(X_test)
    
    # Fit model
    model = LinearRegression()
    model.fit(X_poly, y)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test_poly)
    
    # Calculate MSE
    y_train_pred = model.predict(X_poly)
    mse = mean_squared_error(y, y_train_pred)
    
    axes[idx].scatter(X, y, alpha=0.6, color='steelblue', label='Data')
    axes[idx].plot(X_test, y_pred, 'r-', linewidth=2, label=f'Degree {degree}')
    axes[idx].set_title(f'Polynomial Degree {degree}\nMSE: {mse:.4f}')
    axes[idx].set_xlabel('X')
    axes[idx].set_ylabel('y')
    axes[idx].legend()
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

section: Logistic Regression: From Linear to Probabilistic
text:
Logistic Regression: From Linear to Probabilistic

The Sigmoid Function
For binary classification, we need outputs bounded between 0 and 1. The sigmoid (logistic) function provides this:
\[\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\]
Key properties: - \(\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{R}\) - \(\sigma(0) = 0.5\) (decision boundary) - Derivative: \(\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))\)


The Logistic Regression Model
\[P(y=1|x) = \sigma(\beta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\beta^T x}}\]
The log-odds (logit) is linear:
\[\log\left(\frac{P(y=1|x)}{1 - P(y=1|x)}\right) = \beta^T x\]


Maximum Likelihood Estimation
The likelihood for binary classification:
\[L(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(y^{(i)}=1|x^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} (1 - P(y^{(i)}=1|x^{(i)}))^{1-y^{(i)}}\]
The log-likelihood (to maximize):
\[\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\sigma(\beta^T x^{(i)})) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \sigma(\beta^T x^{(i)})) \right]\]
Equivalently, we minimize the Binary Cross-Entropy Loss:
\[\mathcal{L}(\beta) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]\]


Gradient Descent for Logistic Regression
The gradient of the cross-entropy loss:
\[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{n} X^T (\hat{y} - y)\]
Update rule: \[\beta := \beta - \alpha \frac{1}{n} X^T (\hat{y} - y)\]


Show the Python Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification

# Generate binary classification data
np.random.seed(42)
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=200, n_features=2, n_informative=2, 
                           n_redundant=0, n_clusters_per_class=1, random_state=42)

# Fit Logistic Regression
lr = LogisticRegression(random_state=42)
lr.fit(X, y)

# Create mesh for visualization
x_min, x_max = X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1
y_min, y_max = X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(x_min, x_max, 300),
                     np.linspace(y_min, y_max, 300))

# Predict probabilities
Z_prob = lr.predict_proba(np.c_[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()])[:, 1].reshape(xx.shape)
Z_pred = lr.predict(np.c_[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]).reshape(xx.shape)

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# Probability surface
contour = ax1.contourf(xx, yy, Z_prob, alpha=0.4, cmap='RdBu', levels=20)
ax1.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], c=y, edgecolor='k', cmap='RdBu', s=80, alpha=0.8)
ax1.contour(xx, yy, Z_prob, levels=[0.5], colors='black', linewidths=2, linestyles='--')
ax1.set_xlabel('Feature 1')
ax1.set_ylabel('Feature 2')
ax1.set_title('Probability Surface P(y=1|x)')
plt.colorbar(contour, ax=ax1, label='Probability')

# Decision boundary
ax2.contourf(xx, yy, Z_pred, alpha=0.3, cmap='RdBu')
